title: China Says Door Open to SCS Oil Talks but Demands Philippines 'Show Sincerity'
detail: China's embassy in Manila issued a formal statement on March 26 affirming Beijing's openness to reviving South China Sea joint oil and gas development talks with the Philippines, but conditioning renewed negotiations on Manila demonstrating 'sincerity.' The statement came a day after Philippine DFA Secretary Lazaro confirmed a formal directive from President Marcos to restart energy dialogue could be forthcoming. Analysts noted the exchange hints at an emerging diplomatic opening driven by shared energy pressures — the Iran-linked oil shock has hit both nations' import costs — though fundamental sovereignty disputes and the Philippines' constitutional restrictions on foreign resource access remain unresolved obstacles. President Marcos also confirmed publicly that talks with China on joint exploration were possible, in comments carried by Philstar.

title: Philippines and France Sign Landmark Status of Visiting Forces Agreement in Paris
detail: Philippine Defense Secretary Gilberto Teodoro Jr. and French Armed Forces Minister Catherine Vautrin signed a Status of Visiting Forces Agreement (SOVFA) at the École Militaire in Paris on March 26, making France the first European nation to conclude a visiting forces pact with the Philippines. The agreement was authorized by President Marcos and reached after one year of negotiations. Both sides affirmed the primacy of UNCLOS and the 2016 South China Sea arbitral ruling in the document. The SOVFA will allow French forces to operate from Philippine territory for joint exercises, including anticipated participation in Balikatan 2026. The agreement deepens Europe's direct military engagement in Indo-Pacific security and signals growing European solidarity with Manila on SCS sovereignty.

media.caption: Philippines and France sign landmark Status of Visiting Forces Agreement in Paris

title: Philippines Confirms Gas Strike at Camago-3 Well in Malampaya Fields
detail: President Marcos announced on March 26 that the Philippines successfully struck gas at the Camago-3 well within the Malampaya gas field development area in the South China Sea. The find comes as the Philippines grapples with a declared national energy emergency driven by the Iran war's disruption of Middle East oil supplies. The Malampaya field (Service Contract 38), operated by Prime Energy Resources Development, currently supplies roughly 20% of Luzon's electricity. A new gas discovery at Camago-3 could significantly extend Malampaya's production life, which was forecast to deplete by 2027. The announcement added urgency to discussions about expanding South China Sea energy development, including potential joint exploration with China at Reed Bank.
